section.title: Introduction
section.para: Whenever a VM crashes, it is useful to collect a core dump of the VM memory for debugging and analysis. For physical machines, &kexec; and &kdump; takes care of collecting crash dumps. For virtual machines, it depends on whether the guest is fully virtualized (FV) or paravirtualized (PV).

section.title: Creating crash dumps for fully virtualized machines
section.para: To view crash dumps for FV machines, use the same procedures as for physical machines—&kexec; and &kdump;.

section.title: Creating crash dumps for paravirtualized machines
section.para: Unlike with FVs, &kexec;/&kdump; does not work in paravirtualized machines. Crash dumps of PV guests must be performed by the host tool stack. For &libvirt;-based &vmguest;s, the virsh dump command provides this functionality.

You can configure automatic collection of a core dump with the on_crash setting in the configuration of the &vmguest;. This setting tells the host tool stack what to do if the &vmguest; encounters a crash. The default in &libvirt; is destroy. Useful options for automatically collecting a core dump are coredump-destroy and coredump-restart.

listitem.para: The difference between fully virtualized and paravirtualized virtual machines is described in the virtualization modes reference.
Detailed information about &kexec;/&kdump; mechanism is included in the system tuning documentation.
Refer to for details about the &libvirt; XML settings.
